content: Used `git rebase --onto base feature topic` to move a topic branch across a deleted base. The three-arg form rewrites commits whose parent chain reaches `topic` but not `feature` — diff-by-diff, not by SHA. Verify with `git log --oneline base..HEAD` after; if a commit lands twice it is because the rewrite touched files already in `base`.

`futures::stream::iter(urls).map(fetch).buffered(8)` caps concurrent in-flight tasks at 8 while preserving output order. Switch to `buffer_unordered(8)` when downstream is order-independent — same backpressure, lower tail latency.

In `git rebase -i`, replacing `pick` with `drop` removes a commit entirely. A typo (the letter d alone is drop, but dr is unrecognized) silently keeps the commit if the editor saves with the bad token. After save, re-open the rebase TODO with `git rebase --edit-todo` to sanity-check.

`#[derive(thiserror::Error)]` needs an `#[error(...)]` attribute on every variant or it will not compile. Use `#[from]` on a single inner field per variant for free `From` impls; multiple `#[from]` on one variant is a hard error.

`tokio::select!` cancels every other future the moment one branch resolves. If a branch holds half-finished I/O state, that state is dropped without a chance to recover. Wrap cancellable work in a dedicated task and select on its `JoinHandle` so the work either completes or is explicitly aborted.

Library code returns `Result<T, E>`; only `main`-level glue panics. Following this rule keeps test harnesses from aborting on `unwrap()` deep inside a code path. Marker: if `cargo test` aborts on a single assertion, look for a panicking helper that should have returned an error.

`git commit --fixup <sha>` then `git rebase -i --autosquash main` collapses fixup commits in place. Pair with `[rebase] autoSquash = true` in `~/.gitconfig` so `-i` defaults to autosquash mode and the TODO already has fixup lines pre-positioned.

Custom `axum` extractor returns `Result<MyExt, MyRejection>`. The rejection type must implement `IntoResponse` or the handler will not compile. Easiest path: wrap your error in a struct that implements `IntoResponse` via `(StatusCode, Json<ErrorBody>).into_response()`.

`tokio::spawn(async move { ... })` requires the future to be `Send`. Holding an `Rc` or non-`Send` lock across an `.await` taints the future. Drop the non-`Send` value before the await, or use `spawn_local` on a `LocalSet`.

Replacing `.unwrap()` with `.context(...)?` at boundary functions turns silent panics into structured errors. The pattern: every public fn returns `Result<_, anyhow::Error>`; private helpers can panic on broken invariants only. Reviewers should reject any `unwrap()` in non-test code without a justifying comment.

`git rebase -i HEAD~10` then `:%s/^pick/drop/` in vim drops everything in the range. Useful for pruning a long topic branch back to the last clean ref. Always check `git reflog` first so the dropped commits stay recoverable for ~30 days.

In `tokio::select!`, bias matters. By default the macro evaluates branches in random order each poll for fairness. Use `biased;` at the top of the macro when one branch should win ties (for example, a shutdown signal). Without it, a shutdown can race a unit of work and lose.

`axum::extract::rejection::JsonRejection` already implements `IntoResponse` but emits a generic 400 Bad Request. If the API contract demands a JSON body with `code` and `message` fields, wrap the extractor: `Json<T>` becomes `Result<Json<T>, MyJsonError>` and write the response shape yourself.

`buffered(n)` and `buffer_unordered(n)` only apply backpressure on the producer side. If the consumer is faster than the producer, both behave identically. Profile before tuning `n` upward — common cause of a slow stream is a slow per-item future, not the buffer cap.

`?` propagates errors but loses context unless every boundary attaches it. Use `anyhow::Context::with_context(|| ...)` at each hop, passing a closure that lazily formats the operation being attempted. Reviewing a stack trace later, the layered context lines are what map the failure back to the user-visible operation.

`tokio::task::spawn_blocking` is the safe escape hatch for non-`Send` or blocking work, but it does not propagate cancellation. If the parent task is dropped, the blocking work keeps running on its thread until it returns. Use `tokio::select!` with a `oneshot` cancellation channel inside the closure when this matters.

`#[error(transparent)]` forwards `Display` and `source()` to the inner error. Useful when a variant wraps a third-party error you do not want to rephrase. Pair with `#[from]` on the inner field so the conversion is automatic at `?` sites.

`git commit --fixup=:/keyword` matches the most recent commit whose message contains the keyword. Faster than copy-pasting a SHA when iterating in a long topic branch. Combine with `git rebase -i --autosquash --root` only on private branches — it rewrites history all the way back.

A `select!` branch that resolves to `Err(_)` cancels the rest the same way `Ok(_)` does. If you want to treat partial failure as keep-waiting, convert errors to `Pending` inside the branch (`async { result.ok()?; future::pending().await }`) so the future never resolves on the error path.

Audit pass: replaced every `.unwrap()` in `src/` with `.expect(...)` or `?`. Two of the panics were guarding real malformed-input paths and crashed production once per release cut. The lesson is universal: `unwrap()` in non-test Rust code is a latent crash; promote each to either an explicit `panic!(...)` (for asserted invariants) or `?` propagation.
